questions.question: When did large-scale building activity begin under the Delhi Sultanate after the Turkish conquest of northern India?
questions.explanation: Although by the eighth century CE, Muslims had begun to construct in Sind, Gujarat, etc., it was only in the early thirteenth century that large-scale building activity begun under the Delhi Sultanate.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What is the architectural technique that uses brackets, pillars, and lintels to support a flat roof or small shallow dome?
questions.explanation: Certain techniques and embellishments were prevalent and popular, such as trabeation (brackets, pillars and lintels) to support a flat roof or a small shallow dome.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What structural form was introduced to support the weight of domes, replacing wooden and stone arches?
questions.explanation: Now, however, the archuate form of construction was introduced gradually in which arches could support the weight of the domes.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What are 'voussoirs' in the context of Indo-Islamic architecture?
options: A series of interlocking blocks used to construct arches
questions.explanation: Such arches needed to be constructed with voussoirs (series of interlocking blocks) and fitted with keystones.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What structural elements enabled domes to span large spaces, leaving interiors free of pillars?
questions.explanation: The domes, resting on pendatives and squinches enabled spanning of large spaces leaving the interiors free of pillars.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which forms of art did Muslims develop for their religious architecture since they were forbidden to replicate living forms?
questions.explanation: Muslims, forbidden to replicate living forms on any surface, developed their religious art and architecture consisting of the arts of arabesque, geometrical patterns and calligraphy on plaster and stone.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The architectural styles from Bijapur and Golconda are conventionally categorised under which style?
questions.explanation: The study of Indo-Islamic architecture is conventionally categorised into the Imperial Style... the Provincial Style... the Mughal Style... and the Deccani Style (Bijapur, Golconda).
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of the following is categorised under the Provincial Style of Indo-Islamic architecture?
questions.explanation: The study of Indo-Islamic architecture is conventionally categorised into the... Provincial Style (Mandu, Gujarat, Bengal, and Jaunpur).
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which provincial style borrowed heavily from regional temple traditions like toranas, carvings of bell and chain motifs, and carved panels depicting trees?
questions.explanation: Gujarat was said to have a markedly regional character for patrons borrowed elements from regional temple traditions such as toranas... and carved panels depicting trees, for tombs, mosques and dargahs.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The 15th-century white marble dargah of Shaikh Ahmad Khattu of Sarkhej heavily influenced the form and decoration of which structures?
questions.explanation: The fifteenth century white marble dargah of Shaikh Ahmad Khattu of Sarkhej is a good example of provincial style and it heavily influenced the form and decoration of Mughal tombs.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Where was the lotus bud fringe commonly used to great advantage in Indo-Islamic architecture?
options: In the inner curves of the arches
At the very top of the minars
Around the base of the dome
questions.explanation: The lotus bud fringe was used to great advantage in the inner curves of the arches.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What is the technique of using pictorial mosaic designs with semi-precious stones for surface decoration known as?
questions.explanation: Subsequently the techniques of tessellation (mosaic designs) and pietra dura were made use of for surface decoration particularly in the dado panels of the walls.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: From which century onwards were arches designed with trefoil or multiple foliations?
questions.explanation: From the sixteenth century onwards arches were designed with trefoil or multiple foliations.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: How were the spandrels of the arches typically decorated?
questions.explanation: Spandrels of the arches were decorated with medallions or bosses.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What material was largely used to construct the extremely thick walls of medieval buildings before they were plastered?
questions.explanation: The walls in all buildings were extremely thick and were largely constructed of rubble masonary, which was easily available. These walls were then cased over with chunam...
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which material, utilized from the beginning of the seventeenth century, imparted greater flexibility to the structures?
questions.explanation: From the beginning of the seventeenth century, bricks were also used for construction and these imparted greater flexibility to the structures.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which specific architectural feature at Daulatabad Fort was designed to confound the enemy and prevent elephants from pushing gates open?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad had several strategic devices to confound the enemy, such as staggered entrances so that gates could not be opened even with the help of elephants.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which Mughal emperor was said to have been overawed at the sight of the Gwalior Fort?
questions.explanation: Babur, who did not find much merit in many things he saw in Hindustan, was said to have been overawed at the sight of the Gwalior Fort.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which fort bears the distinction of being the largest fort in Asia?
questions.explanation: Chittorgarh bears the distinction of being the largest fort in Asia and was occupied for the longest length of time as the seat of power.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What was the everyday practical use of the minars attached to mosques?
options: As a watchtower for the military
For the azaan or call to prayer
questions.explanation: The everyday use of the minar was for the azaan or call to prayer.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The Qutub Minar is a 234-feet-high tapering tower divided into how many storeys?
questions.explanation: The Qutub Minar, built in the thirteenth century, is a 234-feet-high tapering tower divided into five storeys.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which revered saint of Delhi became associated with the Qutub Minar?
questions.explanation: The Qutub Minar also came to be associated with the much revered saint of Delhi, Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which fifteenth-century minar boasts of chevron patterning on encaustic tile work and is painted peach?
questions.explanation: Chand Minar, built in the fifteenth century, is a 210- feet-high tapering tower divided into four storeys. Painted peach now, its façade once boasted of chevron patterning on the encaustic tile work...
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: According to Anthony Welch, what was the primary idea behind the monumental tomb structures in medieval India?
options: To mark the center of the city
Eternal paradise as a reward for the true believer
To serve as a fortress in times of war
To store the wealth of the royal family
questions.explanation: According to Anthony Welch, the idea behind the tomb was eternal paradise as a reward for the true believer on the Day of Judgement.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What type of setting were monumental tombs, such as the Taj Mahal and Humayun's tomb, placed within to reflect paradisiacal imagery?
options: A Chahar Bagh or near a body of water
questions.explanation: ...the tomb was, subsequently, placed within paradisiacal elements such as a garden or near a body of water or both, as is in the case of the Humayun's tomb and the Taj Mahal, which follows the charbagh style.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What was the main purpose of sarais in medieval India?
options: To serve as permanent palaces for the king
To provide temporary accommodation to travelers and merchants
To function as local tax collection offices
questions.explanation: Sarais were largely built on a simple square or rectangular plan and were meant to provide temporary accommodation to Indian and foreign travellers, pilgrims, merchants, traders, etc.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Where is the city of Mandu geographically located?
options: Sixty miles from Indore, overlooking the Malwa Plateau and Narmada valley
On the banks of the Yamuna in Agra
questions.explanation: The city of Mandu is located sixty miles from Indore, at an elevation of over 2000 feet and overlooks the Malwa Plateau to the north and the Narmada valley to the south.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Mandu acquired a lot of fame as the capital city of which dynasty founded by Hoshang Shah?
questions.explanation: As the capital city of Ghauri Dynasty (1401-1561) founded by Hoshang Shah it acquired a lot of fame.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Why were the buildings in Mandu designed as arched pavilions that were light and airy?
options: To blend in with Islamic traditions completely
So that these buildings did not retain heat
To allow easy defense against attacks
questions.explanation: In spite of the size or monumentality, the structures were very close to nature, designed in the style of arched pavilions, light and airy, so that these buildings did not retain heat.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which building in Mandu looks like a railway viaduct bridge due to its disproportionately large buttresses?
questions.explanation: The Hindola Mahal looks like a railway viaduct bridge with its disproportionately large buttresses supporting the walls.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What was the original function of the Hindola Mahal in Mandu?
options: The audience hall of the Sultan
questions.explanation: This was the audience hall of the Sultan and also the place where he showed himself to his subjects.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What architectural effect was used effectively to give the walls of Hindola Mahal an impression of swinging?
questions.explanation: Batter was used very effectively to give an impression of swinging (Hindola) walls.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which elegant two-storey 'ship-palace' in Mandu is situated between two reservoirs?
questions.explanation: Jahaaz Mahal is an elegant two-storey 'ship-palace' between two reservoirs, with open pavilions, balconies overhanging the water and a terrace.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Who built the Jahaaz Mahal in Mandu, possibly using it as his harem and ultimate pleasure resort?
questions.explanation: Built by Sultan Ghiyasuddin Khilji it was possibly used as his harem and as the ultimate pleasure and recreational resort.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What kind of structure was the Asharfi Mahal in Mandu, which now lies in ruins?
questions.explanation: A madrasa called Asharfi Mahal now lies in ruins.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which structure in Mandu is regarded as an example of robust Afghan architecture but softened by lattice work, carved brackets, and toranas?
questions.explanation: Hoshang Shah's tomb is a majestic structure... It is regarded as an example of the robustness of Afghan structures, but its lattice work, carved brackets and toranas lend it a softer hue.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What material is the Jama Masjid of Mandu faced with?
questions.explanation: The building is faced with red sandstone.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Who built the Taj Mahal in Agra as a mausoleum for his deceased wife?
questions.explanation: Taj Mahal was built in Agra by Shah Jahan as a mausoleum for his deceased wife Mumtaz Mahal.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Approximately how many specialised workers were employed over nearly twenty years to complete the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: From 1632 onwards it took nearly twenty years and 20,000 specialised workers to complete this monument.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Where is the tomb structure of the Taj Mahal placed within the Chahar Bagh?
options: On the northern extremity to take advantage of the river bank
questions.explanation: The structure is placed on the northern extremity of the bagh instead of the middle to take advantage of the river bank.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What is the height of the four tapering minarets that stand at the corners of the terrace of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: At the corners of the terrace stand four tall, tapering minarets, one hundred and thirty-two feet high.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Where was the white marble used in the Taj Mahal quarried from?
questions.explanation: Marble for the building was quarried from the Makrana mines in Rajasthan and this white edifice is contrasted with the red sandstone of the surrounding structures.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What is the specific geometric shape of the Taj Mahal's tomb structure?
options: A square with chamfers forming eight sides
questions.explanation: The tomb structure is a square with chamfers forming eight sides, recessed with deep arches.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The Gol Gumbad in Bijapur is the mausoleum of which ruler?
questions.explanation: It is the mausoleum of Muhammad Adil Shah (1626-1656), the seventh Sultan of the Adil Shahi Dynasty of Bijapur...
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What gives the Gol Gumbad its nomenclature?
options: A monumental square building topped with a circular drum and a majestic dome
questions.explanation: The Gumbad is a monumental square building topped with a circular drum over which rests a majestic dome, giving the building its nomenclature.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The dome of the Gol Gumbad covers an uninterrupted floor space of 18,337 square feet. Where does this rank globally?
options: The second largest in the world
The third largest in the world
The fourth largest in the world
questions.explanation: The tomb has only one square chamber and the dome, with a diameter of one hundred and twenty-five feet, it covers an uninterrupted floor space of 18,337 square feet, the second largest in the world.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What unique acoustical feature exists along the drum of the dome in the Gol Gumbad?
options: A whispering gallery where sounds get magnified and echoed
A silent chamber that blocks all sound
questions.explanation: The building has an amazing acoustical system. Along the drum of the dome there is a whispering gallery where sounds get magnified and echoed many times over.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In a Jama Masjid, where are the mihrab and the mimbar typically located?
options: In the Qibla Liwan in the west
At the top of the minarets
questions.explanation: Generally, such a mosque was large with an open courtyard, surrounded on three sides by cloisters and the Qibla Liwan in the west. It was here that the mihrab and the mimbar for the Imam were located.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What does the mihrab in a mosque indicate?
options: The location of the Imam's throne
The direction of the Kaaba in Mecca
The resting place of a saint
The place where the Khutba is stored
questions.explanation: People faced the mihrab while offering prayers as it indicated the direction of the Kaaba in Mecca.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: According to the glossary, what is a 'Kos minar'?
options: A decorative niche indicating prayer direction
A distance marker or milestone in the shape and size of a tower
A drum house where ceremonial music was played
questions.explanation: Kos minar: Distance marker or milestone in the shape and size of a tower.
questions.subject: Fine Arts
